channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Stocks Slide as Investors See Rates Rising After Strong Jobs Data
item.description: The S&P 500 fell more than 2.6 percent on Friday, its worst one-day drop of the year and ending nine weeks of gains.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison

item.title: Central Ohio Becomes Hub for Tech and Manufacturing
item.description: Tech titans and Silicon Valley transplants changed the Columbus area, but not everyone is thrilled about the rapid transformation.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times

item.title: U.S. Job Market Pushes Past Shocks and Strains
item.description: Employers added 172,000 jobs in May, adding to a vigorous pace of hiring in recent months. But wage growth is not keeping up with higher prices and consumers remain pessimistic.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis

item.title: Why the SpaceX IPO Will Affect Your 401(k), Like It or Not
item.description: Elon Musk’s rocket company, on the cusp of the largest initial public offering ever, will soon end up in index funds after rule changes by Nasdaq and other index providers.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac and Maureen Farrell
item.media:credit: Olivier Heiligers

item.title: Judge Strikes Down a Slate of Trump’s Immigration Policies
item.description: Also, job growth is looking healthier. Here’s the latest at the end of Friday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Annandale Immigration Court in Annandale, Va., last month.

item.title: Trump Keeps Immunity from I.R.S. Audits, Even as $1.8 Billion Fund Falls Away
item.description: Even as they rebelled against a $1.8 billion fund for President Trump’s allies, Republicans looked the other way as his administration granted him potentially lucrative tax protections.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has long sought to avoid scrutiny of his taxes, and during his first run for office refused to release his tax returns, breaking a norm for presidential candidates.

item.title: Justice Dept. Promises to Drop Trump’s $1.8 Billion Fund
item.description: It was the department’s clearest statement to date that it was pulling back from a plan to use taxpayer money to make payments to people who claimed to have been politically persecuted.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Twin filings on Friday are the strongest signal yet that the Justice Department is serious about dropping the idea of the fund.

item.title: Pentagon Cuts 180 Religious Identities From Military Personnel Records
item.description: The new policy, which the Pentagon framed as a largely administrative action, leaves just 31 religious categories to choose from, 22 of which are Christian.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay, Alexandra E. Petri and Aimee Ortiz
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Mormons (Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints)
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: In a video posted to social media on March 24, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said that the military’s chaplain corps had been “infected by political correctness and secular humanism” under previous administrations.

item.title: Graham Platner Controversy Deepens Democratic Unease Before Maine Senate Primary
item.description: The party’s politicians, officials and strategists wrestled with how to respond to new reporting about Graham Platner, with worries rising about his vulnerabilities in a general election.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Amanda Sabga/Reuters
item.media:description: Graham Platner has been widely expected to win the Democratic primary election for Senate on Tuesday in Maine.

item.title: Several Women Who Dated Graham Platner Recall ‘Unsettling’ Behavior
item.description: The Democratic candidate for Senate in Maine could be charming, women said in interviews, but some found his actions intimidating and disturbing.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck and Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Gabe Souza for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner, a combat veteran, has spoken openly about his past struggles with post-traumatic stress disorder, depression and drinking, and has said his behavior years ago does not reflect who he is today.

item.title: Iran’s Soccer Team Allowed Into U.S. for World Cup, but Many Staff Denied
item.description: After months of doubts over their participation, Iran’s players have received visas for the United States just days before the World Cup begins.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Khalil Hamra/Associated Press
item.media:description: Members of Iran’s national soccer team training in Antalya, Turkey, on Tuesday.

item.title: 38 Tony Nominees Reveal the Strangest Skills They’ve Picked Up
item.description: The stars of “Giant,” “Fallen Angels,” “The Rocky Horror Show,” “Ragtime” and more prove they’ll go to great lengths to be believable in a role.
item.dc:creator: Dana Scruggs, Michael Paulson, Alexis Soloski, Nicole Herrington, Jolie Ruben and Amanda Webster
item.media:credit: Dana Scruggs for The New York Times

item.title: 2026 Tony Awards Predictions: Who Will Win?
item.description: Expect wins for the musical “Schmigadoon!” and the play “Liberation,” and for the “Ragtime” stars Joshua Henry and Caissie Levy.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)

item.title: Man Who Schemed With Au Pair to Kill His Wife Is Sentenced to Life
item.description: Brendan Banfield was found guilty of murdering his wife and another man, who was lured in through a fetish website. The plan involved his lover, who is now serving 10 years in prison.
item.dc:creator: Jacey Fortin and JoAnna Daemmrich
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner/Associated Press
item.media:description: Brendan Banfield arriving to testify in his double murder trial in January in Fairfax, Va.

item.title: First U.S. Screwworm Case Since 1960s Prompts Texas Emergency Response
item.description: After a screwworm infestation was confirmed in a South Texas calf, officials launched an aggressive effort to prevent the parasite from spreading in the nation’s largest cattle-producing state.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Kaylee Greenlee/Reuters
item.media:description: Officials are dispersing millions of sterile screwworm flies in the vicinity of where the infected calf was found.

item.title: ’60 Minutes’ Stars Will Stay After Pelley’s Firing Because They Don’t Want Show to ‘Die’
item.description: Lesley Stahl, Bill Whitaker and Jon Wertheim said on Friday in an email to the show’s staff that they had reached the decision after a period of “grieving” and frustration.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Shannon Stapleton/Reuters

item.title: A Sherpa Survived 6 Days Alone on Everest. His Family Says He Was Abandoned.
item.description: Dawa Sherpa, 57, was found alive on Thursday, nearly a week after he was last seen on the mountain. His wife says more could have been done to find him sooner.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai and Binod Ghimire
item.media:credit: Prabin Ranabhat/Agence France-Presse, via Getty Images

item.title: From Cow-Milking Robots to Weed-Zapping Lasers, Farmers Are Embracing A.I.
item.description: We asked three farmers to tell us how new technology is revolutionizing the way they work.
item.dc:creator: Coralie Kraft and Spencer Lowell
item.media:credit: Spencer Lowell for The New York Times
item.media:description: The machine uses high-resolution cameras to identify weeds.

item.title: What Are A.I. Agents Actually Doing?
item.description: A San Francisco start-up called Arena found that people are most likely to use A.I. agents on the job, particularly if they are in the tech industry.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Brennan Smart for The New York Times

item.title: Wary of U.S., Carney Bets on AI Strategy for Canada
item.description: The country on Thursday released a national artificial intelligence strategy that focuses on building its sovereign capability and protecting consumers.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Chris Young/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada during a demonstration at a Toronto hospital on Thursday. “Canadian A.I. adoption will be prudent, pragmatic and pro-worker,” he said.

item.title: Anthony Head, ‘Buffy the Vampire Slayer’ and ‘Ted Lasso’ Actor, Dies at 72
item.description: The British actor was a mainstay of influential television shows who first found fame as a Nescafe pitchman.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Christian Alminana/Associated Press

item.title: A Question Swirling Around Putin’s Big Conference: Could the War End?
item.description: At the St. Petersburg economic forum, business and political elites said Russia faced a choice of halting the conflict or sacrificing more. Vladimir Putin seemed to signal more war.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:credit: Dmitri Lovetsky/Associated Press
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia speaking at the International Economic Forum in St. Petersburg on Friday.

item.title: ‘Out of Control’: When Troopers and Protesters Clash, Questions Follow
item.description: Gov. Mikie Sherrill had hoped that replacing federal agents with the state police would bring peace. It didn’t work out that way.
item.dc:creator: Chelsia Rose Marcius and Mark Bonamo
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: As night fell outside the Delaney Hall immigration detention center in Newark, clashes between demonstrators and law enforcement officers sometimes ensued.

item.title: Newark Mayor to Scale Back Police Presence at Delaney Hall ICE Detention Center
item.description: The mayor, Ras Baraka, said the city would not spend taxpayers’ money to safeguard Delaney Hall, an immigration detention center in Newark run by a private prison company.
item.dc:creator: Andy Newman
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters gathered outside the Delaney Hall detention facility in Newark on Tuesday. Newark’s mayor said on Thursday that the city’s police force would scale back its presence.

item.title: Refik Anadol’s Dataland: You Feel the A.I. Art, and It Feels You Back
item.description: A look inside Dataland in Los Angeles, dedicated entirely to A.I.-generated art. Refik Anadol, its founder, says it’s for human dreamers. Will critics be convinced?
item.dc:creator: Frank Rose and Sela Shiloni
item.category: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
item.media:credit: Sela Shiloni for The New York Times

item.title: The White House’s Latest Provocation Is Way More Than Just a Troll
item.description: What’s the matter, can’t you take a joke?
item.dc:creator: M. Gessen
item.media:credit: Will Matsuda for The New York Times

item.title: The Betrayal of Black Voters Threatens Our Democracy
item.description: The sweeping effort to dismantle Black-majority congressional districts in the South will have far-reaching consequences for all Americans, and for our democracy.
item.dc:creator: Mara Gay
item.media:credit: Damon Winter/The New York Times

item.title: Driven From Lenapehoking, They Hope to Return to New York
item.description: The Lenape were forced out of their ancestral lands centuries ago. Now in Oklahoma, their descendants feel the pull of homeland in the east.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Maslin Nir
item.category: National Museum of the American Indian
item.media:credit: Elias Williams for The New York Times
item.media:description: Curtis Zunigha, a former chief of the Delaware Tribe of Indians in Bartlesville, Okla., has been making regular trips back to Lenapehoking, “the peoples’ land.”
